summary: This satirical fake news piece mocks eBay and PayPal's notoriously frustrating checkout process by pretending they've patented their convoluted '10-click' buying system. Yegge skewers the disconnect between eBay's 'Buy It Now' promise and the reality of endless password prompts, upsells, and confirmation screens. The piece extends the joke by imagining other rejected patents for equally frustrating UI elements. It's a pointed critique of how dominant platforms can abuse their market position to deliver terrible user experiences without consequence.
key_points: eBay's 'Buy It Now' button is satirized as requiring anywhere from 8-15 clicks to actually complete a purchase
The piece mocks forced sign-outs, password juggling, upselling, and usury-like lending offers as deliberate friction
Other tech companies (Sony PSN, iPad App Store, Firefox) are called out for similarly broken UI promises
The 'monopoly' dig suggests dominant platforms have no incentive to improve user experience
money_quotes: logging in because they signed your sorry ass out again
any purchasing system that lies to you at least nine times about the 'Now' part of 'Buy It Now' is covered by their invention
eBay had tried to patent a purely decorative, non-operational 'Keep me signed in' checkbox
another eBay patent claim for excruciating page load times was rejected because the iPad App Store is still loading
the Firefox browser's 'Do this automatically from now on' checkbox has been complete bullshit for over fifteen years
eBay will continue to innovate on ways to remind their users that monopolies can do whatever the hell they want
eBay's stock rose on the news, driven largely by anonymous shill bidders
key_insight: When companies achieve market dominance, they lose all incentive to respect users' time, turning simple actions into frustrating gauntlets of dark patterns and friction.
